speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 4. Survivor is Evy Woods. The date is April 16, 1996. We left off on the last tape-- you mentioned that the parents of some of the students who had taken you in were in shock because you were considered an illegitimate child. What, what happened? What else happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of them had difficulties with their parents. It was becoming apparent this was not a way for me to stay. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other thing that happened is things were becoming very difficult for my parents, because they insisted on staying together, but on their own, not through places provided by these rescuers. They helped with food. And they helped with some money and ration stamps and things. But they could not provide places. They insisted to them that they should separate. And they would not do it. My father hit on the idea to go as soldier on furlough and rent a room and be alone with his 'girlfriend.' And that seemed to work, except after two or three days you had that form to fill out. Well, they remembered Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], the lawyer of the Levents, who could make all things happen in this universe and would take the forms to him and have him fill them out and get them back. So that worked for a little while. But if you can imagine, you have to be concerned about your haircut, not looking like a vagrant. Your shoes mustn't be walked down, because in that country you keep your shoes orderly, clean, the heels healed. You don't look like a schlumper. The look that you now pay a lot of money for to look like was not in. It was terribly important, because there were also people like Stela Goldschlog, who was a Berlin, German Jew, 21 years old, who had become one of the main informers in that city for the Gestapo. I don't judge it. Yes, no, good, bad, I'm not here for that part. She had a knack for recognizing people living underground. And she herself dressed to the nines, even carried a revolver, had the authority to arrest people on the spot, together with her husband, who was also a Jew. So there is the ugly side, the seamy side of life. And I do like to mention that because that proves an extra jeopardy, your own people-- people like them, there were about five, six of them-- could jeopardize you, in fact, did so, did so in the case of friends of mine who were underground during that same time and did so for my mother and father on one occasion. But she was known by her looks. She was blonde, gorgeous. And she was called the Blonde Poison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but my parents knew who that was. And they were spotted by her. She would sidle up to you and say, I'm also underground, where are you staying? To get you to admit that you were underground. And many people fell for it. And many she recognized by looking at their IDs. Or maybe their look wasn't real.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you haven't been home for a while, imagine for a man, where are you going to get your face shaved every day? Maybe you had to sleep on a park bench, from which you had to move every hour, lest the night police say it's past the hours to be in the park or in this phone booth or wherever people tried to hide. So it was pretty difficult. So what happened next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they made a decision to send me to East Prussia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Abegg, Elisabeth Abegg, and Hilde-- I presume just the two of them and the people involved to make that happen, people I do not know. The church, there was a church called Bekennende Kirche. I do not know what this means in English. It's a particular sect. It wasn't anything weird or, or offbeat. It just was not uncommon in Germany, but you know, the things here have different names. I wouldn't know Methodist in German. Anyway, they must have arranged with the Baroness Von Hulesen, who was living in Hanover, had a big estate in Bloestau, East Prussia, about an hour's train ride, slow train, from Konigsberg. Really rural, that I could stay, we could stay. People had already been, we found out later, staying there, a couple of Jewish people, a, a Nazi woman with her two children who had no place to live. There were just-- the place was so big, she allowed various people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a baroness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Von Hulesen. Actually her son, I'm, I'm told, I was involved in the attempt on Hitler's life, the, the last one with the bomb planted. And when that failed, that attempt, they eventually quartered the German army there on the property, where I landed, because they suspected he would try to escape to his mother's estate and hide in the rural part of Germany. Well, they did not find him there. But we meanwhile had the army on our necks, which was another danger of a different kind. Anyway, it was arranged that I could be sent there. And there was a family staying there, a pastor, who was at the Russian front, his wife, and their three sons, ages 14, 12, and my age, five, by then. And I think I even have a photograph of little Martin and me. Those people were named Bunke, B-U-N-K-E, Frieda Bunke and-- I can't-- he was Pastor Bunke, Pastor. And the children's names, I know. I don't care to say them. I don't know where they are now. The wall is down. And they had been left in East Germany eventually when the war ended. So I would rather not say all that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A woman came to the main train station in Berlin, Anhalter Bahnhof. And she was to accompany me there. It was someone I didn't know. I had no idea who it was, someone that may have been paid to take me there, just as a companion, may not have known anything about us or what was going on here. You couldn't afford to tell people. And that was the last time that I saw my father. He came to the place. I do not remember my mother, but I am sure she was there as well. But I don't remember seeing her or speaking with her or anything. And they said, now, you go. And you go with this nice aunt. They always became immediately aunt somebody. That's how you create intimacy for children, I guess. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was sort of kneeling down talking to me, you know. And all I remember is that he had dark frizzy hair-- that was no help, either, for him-- and the glasses. And I just kept saying yes to everything. And then we went down the steps to where the tracks were, the various tracks to various locations, certain trains that would come in this direction or that. And that was the last time I ever saw him. So that was in June of '43. And I went off with this woman. I don't remember-- it was a fairly long train ride. And we arrived. And we arrived in the big place. And I don't know what became of the woman. I can't even remember how that ended. I just know that the young boy, the small boy, was friendly to me. I didn't think he was one of the brightest children I'd ever seen, sort of a country bumpkin type.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the estate, or the castle, what did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was, it was-- in fact, the workers who would come to the estate to take care of cattle and slaughtering-- I mean, it was a big place-- and the various fruit that was growing, you know, raspberries and boysenberries and gooseberries and all that stuff. It was a huge place. It employed a lot of local people. But the thing was-- the area itself was flat like Kansas. You could see for miles. It was out in nowhere. Podunk, Illinois, sort of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this approximately? Near what large city would this be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Konigsberg. It was about an hour's ride.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eastern Germany, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later it was Eastern Germany. But it was Prussia, East Prussia. You know there's West Prussia and East Prussia. So at one time, at that time it was Germany, East Prussia, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: June '43, still in June '43. And I, I found myself in a fixed bed. And it was the countryside. I'd never been in the countryside. You wore barefoot feet. I mean, you know, I knew nothing about it. And clothes were found, through what means-- I later found out the church really had supplied a lot of those things. And this woman, in turn, took credit for us, as if she was keeping us, which was unfortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What woman are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frau Bunke, who was much older than her husband. She'd been a nurse in World War I. And apparently, he must have been injured. And she cared for him. And this was a star-crossed strange marriage, these two. He was an intellectual, kind, good person. She was grabbing, taking, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] strange woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And still, as best as she knew how to be, she tried to be good. How, how else can I say it? She was in charge of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was arranged for her to take care of me. But she did receive monies and goods in return, which I didn't know that. My mother eventually learned that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what your stay was like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was out of control. The children were out of control. And I was going in that same direction. Some, some bad things happened. The older boys caught diphtheria. I never know how to pronounce it right. A double diphtheria, means on each side. And naturally, I caught it. The boys had scarlet fever. And instead of treating them to the best of one's ability-- scarlet fever makes you itch-- she threw them each a coarse brush and said, go ahead and scratch yourselves as you need. You know, this is a nurse we're talking about. And, of course, I came down with those same things a little later. That was a real problem because hallucinating would take place. And when you hallucinate, you're saying things. You're still ranting about the double carpets in the attic and all this kind of stuff. Meanwhile the 14-year-old boy was coming home and saying, you know-- we'd sit down to eat together. She and the three boys and I. And eventually, my mother came there. She had managed-- After my father was caught in the streets of Berlin trying to sell some of their belongings to turn into some money for food, he had been caught. And that left her on her own. And I don't want to go into all those details, though. She did fine on her own. But it was very depressing and upsetting. And she'd even colored her hair light and taken on a slang accent, because she discovered being by herself was much easier. No one asked for her ID. You know, she was a nice-looking woman, and nobody bothered her. It was different than being together with a man who is under constant suspicion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after spending some nights in the park on the benches and in the phone booths, she had to go back to Dr. Elisabeth Abegg and say, what now? She also tried to do some work from then on, some typing, some knitting, whatever she could to earn some extra money.

speaker: interviewee
text: She helped the greengrocer lady. She stayed there some of the time. She was able to do bookkeeping for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: But always the worry that you mustn't be seen. Who is staying here? Remember, that it is not like here. You could have 20 people in your house if you feel like it. So she got herself-- Elisabeth Abegg had done the best she could. My mother stayed in places and with people that she doesn't even recall, or how many.

speaker: interviewee
text: She got herself a train ticket with money that she had and got the train ticket. Sometimes they'd ask for ID and sometimes not as she was watching. Well, she came up there and she-- I think she chatted up the train master who was selling the tickets and, therefore, got her ticket without her ID possibly and rode on the train supposedly by keeping the lock of the toilet on Unoccupied. When the Gestapo would board the train, they wouldn't bother to look in there. And her seat would be empty. So she rode a lot of the time on that train in the toilet with Unoccupied written there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she arrived and somehow appeared at the door months later. It was in the winter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I, I only remember saying-- and I don't even know why I said that. I have no idea for the motivation. Is it true that my father's dead? Well, I didn't say 'my father.' I said, 'Papi,' which is how you say. It's like daddy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she couldn't understand where I got that. I don't know where I got that. Certainly not from Frau Bunke. She would never have talked about anything like that. And so my mother stayed there and tried to civilize me. I had snot nose right down to the lip here. And my hair was filthy and dirty. But their own children were filthy and dirty. It wasn't something about me. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- my mother was afraid to eat because the woman would always complain they didn't have enough. But the dog would get a frying pan full of potatoes and, and bacon and eggs and-- it was foods we hadn't seen in years. Well, I did not know all this was happening, of course, but my mother did. And that was where eventually then the army was quartered, because the order came. You must move westward and not throw yourself at the Russians that are approaching. And she said, I'll tell you what, when you leave, I'll attend to all the slaughter and have all that meat packed and sent to you, where you're going to some-- I think she was going back to her family into that forest area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are you talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frau Bunke and her little family. See, they had to obey orders like that. And so my mother said she would attend to that and did. But slaughtering is a tough business if it isn't something you've always seen and done. I still hear pigs squealing in my ears. I hate that. Of course, we wouldn't eat that stuff either. But my mother did take care of that for them and have that meat sent. We did meet Pastor Bunke once. He came home on Christmas leave. It had to have been in '43. He was a really wonderful, nice man. He was like a cool compress on your head. And during those holidays, those few days of leave he had, he took us to the church. And my mother had already taught me you're not supposed to be comfortable singing some Christmas song or thing. It's not our song. But we went to the church, and the pastor or minister there spoke to the teeny, little congregation there on Christmas and to us, as if he knew about us. And that scared my mother so terribly that we had to get out of there. But he didn't say anything disparaging. He was trying to tell us that everything-- not everyone is doing what's happening out there. He was really trying to give encouragement. I think my mother once said Jehovah's Witnesses came to their door like that, too, and they were scared they were being arrested. It was Jehovah's Witnesses saying-- taking a chance on their lives. We've been big fans of Jehovah's Witnesses ever since. [DRINKS] But we stayed there together. Except the Nazi woman proved a problem. She had two little girls. And the two little girls were-- one was, I think, a year older than I. The other one about three years old. The three-year-old one had black hair and black eyes, the epitome of what the Hitler regime would have called a very Jewish-looking child. In fact, in our little photograph, she's the only one who looks like a Jewish little child, which is so funny. But it wasn't. During play, my mother would sit and knit with this woman. She couldn't get rid of this woman. The mother of these children would badger and look for her, badger her to come sit with her, because you're the only intelligent person to talk to here. And I have a degree in racial studies. And she knew Jews at a 100-meter distance. All I know is any time I was around that woman, my mother told me I couldn't talk, not one word of anything. I was to be quiet. Well, the two children and I were playing in the sandbox. And I still remember the sandals that I had on that day. In those times the sandals had a division or two in the soles. So you had to be careful your feet didn't get pinched in as you walked. [LAUGHS] We were playing. And I suddenly got up and said to her, well, I have to tell you something-- whispered-- and my mother said, it is rude to whisper. You know, here's more of that German discipline stuff. And I said, but I know I have to whisper this. I know this. The woman finally felt sorry for me, I guess, and said to my mother, it's all right. Let her whisper. And I whispered to her, and I still remember it. It was a sunny day. They sat under an umbrella. I remember the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, can I-- while we're playing in the sandbox, can I just once say my name, Goldstein? Thought she was going to fall out of her chair. And she turned around and did what-- probably the only thing that she could think of-- and that was to say, don't you ever-- and with the finger to this day-- don't you ever say a word like that again. You know, as if I had said some obscene word. You had to be quick-witted to handle these circumstances.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but I was happy and went back. I had said what I needed to say. And the woman never caught on. Nor did she ever identify my mother, although she could tell Jews at 100 meters, had been trained to. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we stayed there until the Bunkes had to leave. The woman finally bailed out. The army came trying to look for that young man. My mother did some helping with the army men. The army men were actually OK. They already knew. This-- they were done with the whole war already. A couple of them, the older ones, the officers, were Nazis. But getting weaker willed, they had planned to do the top dog in as soon as they could and did eventually on the premises. Put him in a smoke room on a hook, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did they-- what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their own-- one of their own commanding officers, who was a top dog Nazi, the enlisted men strung up, planned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: On this farm? On this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, in the smoke house where we would put the meat.

words.text: house where we would put the meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I knew of the smoke house. But I didn't see them. Huh uh.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had to leave there. That was just too dangerous for us there. And then there'd be inquiries. The mayor had come around. Why-- how old is this child? And why was I not in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I also had to pretend I was a different age, because, you see, I was school age at, at this point. And so because I was very little, thank heavens, it was easy to say I was a year younger. But you know, children know when-- how old they are. You can't fool them so easily. But again, it worked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the mayor would come from town, we'd have to go out in the field and stand in the soggy rain where all the, the hay had been cut, you know, over the stubbles. I still remember that. And I'd say to my mother, why are we out here? It is raining. We don't have rain clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was waiting for that horse and cart to leave. And every so often she was sure we were done for. And then it finally happened. She had-- we knew we had to leave. It was no longer safe. There was a woman who was with the army somehow who also lived in that place, a big party member. She-- my mother heard her making a phone call saying, I have asked you to inquire about this woman that lives here, and-- Frau Holstein. You still haven't gotten a report back to me. Something is not right. She has a Berlin accent. See, this is one of the things. You have a way of speaking from different regions. And this was in the country where they nearly spoke a flat German like the Dutch people do. You almost couldn't tell the two apart. And when my mother heard that, she knew we had to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what were we going to do? You know, there were all kinds of problems that we had overcome. I'd had all those diseases and the delirium and the doctor, the Nazi doctor who was called, who looked at me, and Frau Bunke pretended I was her little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I can't help this child. My own son died of this last week. There's no medication for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we managed to get a shipment from Dr. Abegg in Berlin. Can you believe it? And that helped with the problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we had been through all kinds of stuff. You couldn't live. You couldn't die. My mother was sitting one, I guess, early afternoon in her room, which was our room in that big, big place. And there was a woman who used to come often and bring socks for the soldiers that she had, socks that she had mended, knitted, whatever. And she was a really, really sweet person. I'm trying to think of what her name was. Could have been Frau Mischke. And she had a face kind of like Dr. Elisabeth Abegg. You know, some people already radiate a kind of goodness. I can't explain it to you. But it's true. She asked what the problem was. And in the dark there, my mother said, well, I have this friend, and her daughter. And here's the problem. And they need to leave. They need to find a place to stay. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess she may have told them that the friend was a-- were Jewish. And the woman said, it's really you, isn't it? Well, my mother didn't know if she was about to be murdered or if she had found help.

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman said, I'll take care of it. And did. And so on another little train ride, another hour with one of those little slow trains that are out in the country, we had to go to a place called Gamsau, and that was a sharecropper woman who looked twice as old as she really was. Husband was at the front. She had a mob of children from small to adult and married. A daughter-in-law was staying there with her two, two children. We were all in this little hut, you know, with no electricity, no toilet. We had chickens. And we had milk. And this woman was the first person in all of these events who treated my mother the way you would treat a loved person or a daughter. I remember my mother had nobody to talk to in all those years, even the helpers. You couldn't afford to talk about anything to anyone. And my mother was sick by this point. She had a lot of chronic diarrhea. She had a lot of gall bladder attacks. She had all kinds of problems. And this woman got her the warmest place to sleep in the house, namely in the kitchen by the stove.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this woman's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, isn't funny? Now I know how my mother must have felt when she couldn't remember names. Subitski, with an S. Subitski. And Subitski's daughter finally stole a pin, because people at the public water pump-- this is in the sticks I'm talking about-- would notice that my mother wasn't wearing a party thing from the NSF, which was the national women's organization. And the 16-year-old daughter, who supposedly didn't know very much about us, came running from the train on one of her shopping errand things and said, look what I brought for you. You put this on. And that will shut their mouths for a while. And it did. Trouble was, again, we had to avoid speaking because of that Berlin accent. They would have known that at the pump. We had to learn to wear the wooden clogs with the pigskin tops and the frozen ice and all this stuff. Bathing, I don't recall. I think it was a low priority thing. But it was good training for what followed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mama Subitski was a really wonderful person. And we really stayed there with intermittent frights and events that threatened to expose us, where we had to stand behind doors while the postman came. And one child would have postal viewing duty. You stare out the little tiny window of this cottage. This was a tiny place. I don't know how we all managed to sleep there. But we did. Must have been about eight, nine of us-- to say, OK, the so-and-so is coming, the mayor from the nearby town or the postman is coming. And then we had to go in the potato cellar real quick, see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we became familiar with that. But we were already starting to store up rations in case the Russians came, potatoes and all these important things. And I remember that was the first time I had been in snow. Berlin is not a particularly heavy snowfall city. It gets a little bit, and it turns to dirt. Well, there was tons of snow there. And the little lake was frozen over right by the property. But remember, they did not even own this. They were poor as dirt. They were sharecroppers. And you know what that means. That means you get to keep what you need to eat. And that's it.
